result.markdown:
--- title: "What should I study to get better at Jeopardy?" description: "Scraping 40 years of J-Archive, then clustering 123k categories to find what to learn." date: "2026-07-06" categories: [data science, nlp, jeopardy] image: thumbnail.png draft: true --- I like Jeopardy, and I wanted a data-driven answer to a simple question: of everything the show could ask me, *what is actually worth studying?* This post walks through how I scraped 40 years of the show, turned every category into something a computer can compare, grouped the categories into "types," named them, and used the result to find where studying pays off. Every figure below is generated from a committed dataset — no live scraping — so the whole post re-renders from scratch. ## Scraping J-Archive There's no API, so the data comes from a polite, cached crawl of [J-Archive](https://j-archive.com): a `fetch` stage (one request/second, every page cached to disk), a `parse` stage that reads each game's board out of the HTML, and a resumable `crawl` that walks every season and game. The raw crawl stays on my machine; what ships is a single compact `clues.parquet` — one row per clue — so anyone (including the CI that builds this site) starts from identical data without re-scraping. The result is **563,266 clues** across **9,485 games**, spanning 1984 to 2026. ::: {#cell-fig-overview .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4)) per_year = clues["air_date"].dt.year.value_counts().sort_index() axes[0].bar(per_year.index, per_year.values, color="#4C72B0") axes[0].set_title("Clues per year") gt = clues["game_type"].value_counts().head(6)[::-1] axes[1].barh(gt.index, gt.values, color="#55A868") axes[1].set_title("Clues by game type (top 6)") rounds = clues["round"].value_counts() axes[2].bar(rounds.index, rounds.values, color="#C44E52") axes[2].set_title("Clues by round") axes[2].tick_params(axis="x", rotation=20) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![The corpus: clues per year, game types, and rounds.](index_files/figure-html/fig-overview-output-1.png){#fig-overview width=1418 height=430} ::: ::: ## From categories to documents To compare categories, each one becomes a short text "document": its title followed by its clue → answer pairs (in a shuffled order, so no clue position carries special weight). For example, one category instance reads: ::: {#sample-doc .cell execution_count=3} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` FISH, BIRD OR MAMMAL. The cormorant -> a bird; The macaque -> a mammal; The stickleback -> a fish; The pangolin -> a mammal ``` ::: ::: Each document is embedded into a 384-dimensional vector with a local sentence-transformer (`bge-small-en-v1.5`) — a step heavy enough (a GPU-less few minutes over 123k documents) that it runs offline and commits its *output*, never re-running when the post builds. ## Clustering into "types" Running K-means (k = 50) over those vectors groups the ~123k category instances into recognizable *types*. Projected to 2D with UMAP, the map looks like this — hover any point to see its type, an example category, and that type's most common entity: ::: {#cell-fig-clusters .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} frac = min(1.0, 10000 / len(clusters)) samp = clusters.groupby("cluster_id", group_keys=False)[clusters.columns.tolist()].apply( lambda g: g.sample(frac=frac, random_state=42) ) samp["type"] = samp["cluster_id"].map(labels) top1 = tokens[tokens["rank"] == 1].set_index("cluster_id")["phrase"].to_dict() samp["top_entity"] = samp["cluster_id"].map(top1).fillna("—") fig = px.scatter( samp, x="umap_x", y="umap_y", color="type", hover_name="type", hover_data={"category": True, "top_entity": True, "umap_x": False, "umap_y": False, "type": False, "cluster_id": False}, render_mode="webgl", opacity=0.55, ) fig.update_traces(marker={"size": 4}) fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, height=620, margin={"l": 0, "r": 0, "t": 10, "b": 0}, xaxis_title=None, yaxis_title=None) fig.show() ``` ::: {#fig-clusters .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` 123k category instances, grouped into 50 types (hover to explore; ~10k-point sample). ::: ::: The 50 types are far from equal in size: ::: {#cell-fig-sizes .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} sz = summary.copy() sz["type"] = sz["cluster_id"].map(labels) sz = sz.sort_values("size").tail(25) plt.figure(figsize=(8, 9)) plt.barh(sz["type"], sz["size"], color="#4C72B0") plt.xlabel("category instances") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![How common each category type is.](index_files/figure-html/fig-sizes-output-1.png){#fig-sizes width=875 height=979} ::: ::: ## Naming the 50 types Each cluster is labelled automatically with a three-part "fingerprint": its most frequent real category names, the instances closest to its centre, and its most distinctive words (via class-based TF-IDF). Reading those fingerprints, the 50 types name themselves: ::: {#tbl-types .cell execution_count=6} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ```{=html}
Type Categories Example entities
0 Wordplay & Vocabulary 5064 Idiomatically, the Greek
1 Pop Music & Songs 4230 Grammy, Elton John
2 Books & Authors 4113 Jane Eyre, Willa Cather
3 Rhymes, Anagrams & Common Bonds 4077 Alabama Institute, Is For
4 Movies 3773 Best Picture, Jack Nicholson
5 Notable People & Awards 3760 Thurgood Marshall, Chief Justice
6 Word Origins & Foreign Words 3669 Yiddish, the Greek
7 Food & Drink 3426 Joy of Cooking, Parmesan
8 U.S. Geography 3344 Carson City, Juneau
9 Animals & Zoology 3124 Madikwe Game Reserve, Galapagos Islands
10 Literature & Fictional Characters 3111 Jane Eyre, of the Flies
11 World Geography & Waters 3100 Caspian Sea, Lake Nicaragua
12 American History 2987 Fort Sumter, Jefferson Davis
13 Television 2979 Emmy, Emmys
14 Potpourri / Grab-Bag 2911 Old Sturbridge Village, Bake Oven
15 World History & Leaders 2858 Pol Pot, Helmut Kohl
16 Museums, Landmarks & Travel 2828 Space Needle, Space Museum
17 Puzzle & Grab-Bag Categories 2825 Disaster City, Sony
18 Royalty & European History 2794 William the Conqueror, Isabella
19 Oscars & Movie Quotes 2713 Best Picture, Leonard Maltin
20 Names & Nicknames 2702 Benjamin Siegel, of Humbugs
21 Countries & Languages 2654 Suriname, Liechtenstein
22 U.S. Presidents 2631 Woodrow Wilson, Republican
23 Sports 2425 World Series, National League
24 Medicine & The Body 2395 Langerhans, Eustachian
25 Law, Economics & Abbreviations 2222 Hatchett, Amendment
26 Fashion & Furnishings 2186 Dior, Ralph Lauren
27 Religion & The Bible 2172 Isaiah, Saul
28 World Capitals & Cities 2094 Brasilia, Jakarta
29 Explorers & Ships 2094 Francis Drake, Gama
30 Business & Brands 2067 Berkshire Hathaway, Procter
31 Chemistry & Earth Science 2050 Hg, Mohs
32 Opera & Classical Music 2046 Verdi, Puccini
33 Children's Literature 1936 Little Bo Peep, Pinocchio
34 TV Sitcoms & Families 1930 Frasier, Modern Family
35 Famous Women & First Ladies 1880 Abigail Adams, First Lady
36 Quotes & Proverbs 1825 Nietzsche, Kierkegaard
37 Inventors & Scientists 1755 Edison, Pasteur
38 Mythology & The Ancient World 1704 Athena, Odysseus
39 Theatre & Broadway 1605 Tony, Tonys
40 Poetry 1602 Emily Dickinson, Robert Frost
41 Plants & Botany 1598 Dionaea, Acer
42 Art & Artists 1592 Lautrec, Gauguin
43 Games & Play 1489 Scrabble, Clue
44 Transportation & Cars 1349 Honda, Toyota
45 Astronomy & Space 1194 Saturn, Pluto
46 Holidays & The Calendar 1154 Columbus Day, Veterans Day
47 Math & Measurement 1082 Celsius, Venn
48 Colleges & Universities 952 University of Pennsylvania, Ivy League
49 Shakespeare 883 Othello, Macbeth
``` ::: ::: ## What the data says to study Two findings stand out. First, **wordplay is the single biggest meta-domain** — homophones, anagrams, rhymes, hidden words, "___-letter words." Grouped together, roughly one category in ten rewards a *skill* you drill rather than facts you memorize. Push the clustering finer and wordplay even splits into two prep tracks: pure letter/anagram games (drill) versus etymology and idioms (genuinely studyable). Second, the types differ enormously in whether "study the common answers" even *works*. We can measure it: how many distinct entities recur ≥5 times in a type. Entity-dense types (authors, geography, presidents) are eminently studyable; wordplay and grab-bag types are not: ::: {#cell-fig-applicability .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} ap = (tokens.groupby("cluster_id")["n_qualifying_phrases"].first().reset_index()) ap["type"] = ap["cluster_id"].map(labels) ap = ap.sort_values("n_qualifying_phrases") top = ap.tail(12); bottom = ap.head(8) show = pd.concat([bottom, top]) plt.figure(figsize=(8, 9)) colors = ["#C44E52"] * len(bottom) + ["#55A868"] * len(top) plt.barh(show["type"], show["n_qualifying_phrases"], color=colors) plt.xlabel("distinct recurring entities (count >= 5)") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![How studyable each type is: distinct entities recurring 5+ times.](index_files/figure-html/fig-applicability-output-1.png){#fig-applicability width=879 height=979} ::: ::: And for the studyable types, here are the entities that actually recur — the stuff worth knowing: ::: {#tbl-entities .cell execution_count=8} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
U.S. Presidents count
0 Woodrow Wilson 113
1 Republican 160
2 Gerald Ford 119
3 Martin Van Buren 65
4 Andrew Jackson 119
5 Grover Cleveland 80
6 John Quincy Adams 99
7 Taft 94
8 Andrew Johnson 88
9 John Adams 127
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
World Geography & Waters count
0 Caspian Sea 76
1 Lake Nicaragua 24
2 Bay of Bengal 62
3 Black Sea 106
4 Indian Ocean 126
5 Bay of Biscay 41
6 Volga 61
7 Great Lake 79
8 Lake Baikal 43
9 Persian Gulf 81
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Books & Authors count
0 Jane Eyre 63
1 Willa Cather 70
2 Thomas Hardy 81
3 Agatha Christie 111
4 Sinclair Lewis 88
5 Red Badge of Courage 49
6 Toni Morrison 65
7 Edith Wharton 48
8 Madame Bovary 46
9 Stephen Crane 52
``` ::: ::: ## A study tool The natural next step is a lookup tool: pick a category type, see its most common entities, and jump straight to Wikipedia for each. That interactive research page is being built next — it will live at `research/` (built next).
